column3: The use case is to create a clear, well‑structured article starting from rough bullet points or early notes. You begin with your own raw ideas and then use Copilot to turn those ideas into a polished story suitable for ASML audiences. By providing Copilot with your initial notes, Copilot transforms them into a coherent article based on your guidance (audience, word count, tone). After gathering peer feedback, you feed that back into Copilot, which quickly generates improved and alternative versions, speeding up review cycles and enhancing the final quality.
column4:
Step 1 - Write a short outline with your early thoughts: bullet points, loose notes, target audience, and the message you want to convey. Copilot doesn't need a full story, just enough context to understand your direction.
“Here are my bullet points. Create a draft article that follows my intended direction.”

Step 2 - Feed your notes plus a specific prompt indicating word count, audience, and any sources. The clearer your instruction, the more accurate and usable the article.

“Turn these bullet points into a clear, well‑structured article with logical flow for a non‑technical ASML audience.”

Step 3 - Ask colleagues for input on tone, clarity, or structure, then provide their comments back to Copilot. You can request multiple improved versions to choose the best fit.

“Apply this feedback and generate 3 revised versions with slightly different professional tones.”
column6: Make sure you are aware of responsible AI use and validate output.

column3: This use case demonstrates how Copilot Chat can accelerate data analysis workflows, generate meaningful insights, and improve communication quality. It outlines a step-by-step process in which users upload data, ask Copilot to analyze and visualize it, and refine their interpretations and written communication.
column4:
Step 1 – In Excel, analyze data with Copilot using this prompt:

“Open this CSV file and perform an exploratory data analysis. Summarize key patterns, outliers, and trends in clear bullet points.”

Step 2 – Generate visualizations instantly using this prompt:

“Create a column chart showing the trend over time. Use winsorization to handle extreme outliers before generating the visualization.”

Step 3 – Refine insights and communication using this prompt:

“Rewrite this message to make it clearer, more professional, and easier to understand. Highlight any phrases that may cause ambiguity.”
column6:
Risk: Uploading sensitive or confidential data.
Mitigation: Be responsible when uploading files. Only use approved, non‑confidential datasets and follow organizational data‑handling policies.

Risk: Misinterpretation of Copilot’s analysis or visualizations.
Mitigation: Validate all insights against the original dataset and apply your own analytical judgment.

Risk: Ambiguous or incorrect refinements of written communication.
Mitigation: Review Copilot’s edited text for context accuracy and ensure it aligns with the intended message.

Risk: Over‑reliance on Copilot outputs.
Mitigation: Use Copilot as a support tool, not a substitute for critical thinking; cross-check results as needed.

column2: Setting up Lead Time, P85, and 4-Week Rolling Sprint Dashboard in Jira
column3: Teams lack visibility into true feature lead time, cycle predictability (P85), and rolling sprint performance, resulting in poor forecasting accuracy and difficulty identifying bottlenecks. Copilot helps in generating optimized JQL queries, interpreting data fields and automating the creation of dashboard components. Users can ask Copilot Chat to validate lead time definitions, build filters for P85 percentile analysis, and generate rolling 4-week throughput visuals. This results in faster dashboard creation and fewer manual data errors, better continuous improvement and increased predictability and visibility.
column4: Step 1 - In Excel, analyze data with Copilot using this prompt: “Compare average cost and lead time of aluminum vs titanium for part RC-CLMP-2025.”
column6: Be aware of responsible AI use guidelines. To use Copilot in this way you need: Jira Software access with project permissions, consistent workflow and status transitions, and historical ticket data.

column3: Use Copilot Chat to speed up the cost analysis process by uploading your data file and prompting efficiently.
column4:
Step 1 - Upload your Excel data file to Copilot Chat and let it generate the formula's needed by prompting: "Summarize the total operating expenses for each country using the data uploaded. Group the results by country and show the output as a summary table." 

Step 2 - Easily ask Copilot Chat for the top cost categories by prompting: "Identify the top 3 countries with the highest total operating expenses. Use the 'Operating Expenses' column, aggregate the spending by country and total operating expenses." 

Step 3 - Copilot Chat can help with filtering outcomes and grouping into specific categories by prompting: "Filter the data in "Cost Analysis' sheet to show only records where the country 'US' and the profit is negative. group filtered results by month and display a table with Month, Profit, and Operating Expenses."
column6: Make sure you are aware of the information that can responsibly be shared with Copilot Chat.

column3:
Before using Copilot, development was slowed down by manual project setup, repetitive boilerplate coding, and constant dependency troubleshooting. Developers spent significant time recalling framework-specific syntax and resolving configuration issues instead of focusing on building features. These inefficiencies increased cognitive load, extended delivery  timelines, and impacted the productivity of inexperienced team members.

The team needs to develop a full-stack Comments Tool application using Angular 21 and Spring Boot 4 that supports secure authentication via OKTA, integrates with an Oracle database, and provides a clean, consistent UX based on provided design screens. 
The solution must include three routed pages (Intake, Comments, Comment Detail), support local development using H2 DB and authentication bypass, and follow modern clean-code and architectural best practices. A monorepo structure, Dockerized modules, well-defined domain models, and backend unit tests are required to ensure maintainability, scalability, and efficient collaboration across the team.
column4: Step 1 - In Excel, analyze data with Copilot using this prompt: “Compare average cost and lead time of aluminum vs titanium for part RC-CLMP-2025.”
column6: Make sure you stay the human in the loop, reviewing all the code that is generated by Copilot Chat. In addition, make sure you are aware of the responsible AI use guidelines of ASML.

column3:
This use case shows how M365 Copilot can be used to review and improve the quality of Root Cause Analyses (RCAs) for major IT incidents.
Copilot provides structured, standards‑based RCA quality feedback to RCA authors and reviewers by evaluating RCAs against ITIL4, the full 8D methodology (D1–D8), NxWhys causal depth, and ASML T / P / S corrective‑action standards.
The outcome is more consistent, evidence‑based, and auditable RCAs, supporting systemic learning and reducing recurrence risk.
column4:
Step 1 – Input (Copilot Chat / Word)

The user pastes or uploads a draft or final RCA document into M365 Copilot.

Step 2 – RCA Quality Review (Copilot Chat)
Copilot is instructed to act as a Senior IT Problem Management Auditor and performs a deterministic RCA quality review covering:

  • D1–D8 alignment (8D methodology)
  • Root cause depth and escape‑point validation (NxWhys)
  • Corrective‑action quality using ASML T / P / S categories
  • Evidence‑based reasoning only (no speculation or personal attribution)

Example prompt:
“Review this RCA using ITIL4, 8D, NxWhys, and ASML T/P/S standards. Identify gaps, validate root cause depth, assess corrective actions, and provide a structured RCA quality assessment.”

Step 3 – Output (Copilot Response)
Copilot generates a deterministic, audit‑ready RCA quality report, including:

  • RAG and STAR scorecards
  • Strengths and gaps per 8D step
  • Root cause to corrective‑action traceability
  • Actionable feedback to improve RCA quality

Step 4 – Usage
The output is used by RCA authors and reviewers for RCA review, governance, quality assurance, and continuous improvement of RCA practices.
column6:
Risk: Over‑reliance on AI output
Mitigation:
Copilot is used strictly as an RCA quality review and feedback tool. Final RCA ownership, validation, and approval remain with RCA authors and reviewers.

Risk: Sensitive information in RCA content
Mitigation:
Users follow existing ASML information‑handling and responsible‑use guidelines. Copilot processes only user‑provided RCA content.

column2: Forecasting Sales and Costs using Python
column3: Copilot Chat can analyze sales and cost data with Python-based forecasting on existing Excel data. The prompts used can be found in the attached screenshots. Through those prompts Copilot Chat can generate forecasts for sales and costs with clear assumptions, trend analysis, and anomaly detection reports. Lastly, with these insights Copilot Chat can generate drafts of executive summaries to translate results into insights, risks and recommendations
column4:
Step 1 - Analyze sales and cost data using Copilot Chat with Python-based forecasting on existing Excel data

Step 2 - Generate forecasts for sales and costs with clear assumptions, trend analysis, and anomaly detection.

Step 3 - Generate executive summaries to translate results into insights, risks and recommendations
column6: Make sure a specialist remains the human in the loop and that you are aware of the responsible use of AI and specifically Copilot Chat. For information on this, go to the SharePoint of AI@ASML.

column2: Folder-based Email Reporting Automation with Python + Copilot
column3:
This use case complements Outlook Copilot by processing a target Outlook folder in batch and generating a structured, date-based digest for project follow-up and manager reporting.

While Outlook Copilot is effective for understanding and replying to individual email threads, this workflow helps users review many project-related emails at once, extract the most relevant sections, and convert them into a reusable summary format.

This matters because project or coordination mailboxes often contain repeated updates, action items, and open issues spread across multiple emails and dates. A batch digest reduces manual review time and improves reporting consistency.
column4:
Step 1 – In Outlook, select a target project or coordination folder and define the review period.

Step 2 – In Python, collect emails from the selected Outlook folder and apply filters such as date range, sender, subject, or keyword.

Step 3 – In Python, generate an HTML digest that organizes the filtered emails with fields such as date, sender, subject, folder, and extracted description text.

Step 4 – In Microsoft 365 Copilot, review the HTML digest using a prompt such as:
“Summarize this mailbox digest by date and identify the main topics discussed.”

Step 5 – In Microsoft 365 Copilot, refine the summary using a prompt such as:
“Create a manager-ready update with these sections: Main topics, Action items, Current status, Risks, and Next steps. Write it in Korean.”

Step 6 – In Windows Task Scheduler, automate the Python workflow to generate the digest on a recurring basis.

Output – A structured, date-based mailbox digest that supports project follow-up and manager reporting.
column6:
Risk: Sensitive or internal email content may be exposed if real mailbox data is shared in examples or reports.
Mitigation: Use anonymized or redacted examples only. Do not include real employee names, email addresses, project identifiers, ticket numbers, hostnames, site codes, or internal links in the submitted use case.

Risk: Copilot-generated summaries may be incomplete or inaccurate.
Mitigation: All summaries must be reviewed and validated by a human before being shared or used for reporting.

Risk: Users may assume this replaces Outlook Copilot or existing mailbox review practices.
Mitigation: Position this workflow as a complement to Outlook Copilot, focused on batch processing of a target folder for digest/report generation rather than single-thread summarization.
